__data__.text: Article Inﬂuence of Physical Effects on the Swarming Motility of Pseudomonas aeruginosa Alexander Yang, 1 Wai Shing Tang, 2 Tieyan Si, 3 and Jay X. Tang 1,* 1Physics Department, Brown University, Providence, Rhode Island; 2Department of Physics, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Hong

ABSTRACT Many species of bacteria can spread over a moist surface via a particular form of collective motion known as  surface swarming . This form of motility is typically studied by inoculating bacteria on a gel formed by 0.4–1.5% agar, which contains essential nutrients for their growth and proliferation. Using Pseudomonas aeruginosa and its pili-less mutant, DPilA, we investigate physical factors that either facilitate or restrict the swarming motility, measured by the rate of increase in area covered by a spreading bacterial colony, i.e., a swarm. The wild-type colony spreads over the agar surface in highly branched structures. The pili-less mutant ﬁlls up the area more fully as it spreads, but it also produces numerous and fragmented branches, or tendrils, at the swarm front.

Whereas additional surfactants enhance swarming, increasing the agar percentage, adding extra salt or sugar or incorporating viscous agents in the agar matrix all decrease swarming, supporting the conclusion that swarming motility is restricted by the surface tension at the swarm front and swarm growth is limited by the rate of water supply from within the agar gel. The physical basis elaborated through this study provides a useful framework for understanding the swarming behavior of numerous species of bacteria.

Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a Gram-negative, rod-shaped bacterium that employs both a single ﬂagellum and type IV pili for motility in a wide variety of environments. It is a common opportunistic pathogen. Although P . aeruginosa infection in healthy individuals is rare, it is the primary cause for pulmonary infection and early death in cystic ﬁbrosis pa- tients (1). In addition, hospitalized patients with endotracheal tubes or indwelling venous or urinary catheters are at high risk for nosocomial infection by P . aeruginosa (2) because it is able to spread over moist surfaces in a process called ‘ ‘swarming’ ’. Swarming typically involves collective motion of a dense population of bacteria. It is a common mode of motility that allows bacteria to rapidly colonize a surface, leading to the formation of bioﬁlms ( 3).

Bacterial bioﬁlms are associated with increased antibiotic resistance ( 4), and are estimated to contribute to up to 65–80% of all microbial infections (5). To move through their environments, bacteria employ a plethora of methods. The use of ﬂagella is seen in both swimming motility (in liquids) and swarming motility (on wet surfaces) ( 6). When bacteria transition from swimming to swarming, they encounter more viscous drag, which re- quires greater torque from the ﬂagellar motor. Some bacte- ria, such as Escherichia coli and Salmonella typhimurium , undergo hyperﬂagellation as they switch to swarming motility ( 7). P . aeruginosa, on the other hand, has two different motor stators, either of which is sufﬁcient for swimming motility, but both are required for swarming motility ( 8).

Another intrinsic difference between swim- ming and swarming is that swimming takes place individu- ally, while swarming requires a concerted multicellular effort. For P . aeruginosa, swarming assays are usually done using 0.4–1% agar. Higher agar percentages generally prohibit swarming, although some species of bacteria, such as Paenibacillus dendritiformis, can swarm on >1.5% agar (9); lower agar percentages ( %0.4%) allow motile bacteria to inﬁltrate the agar gel, as the polymer network forming the gel becomes sufﬁciently weak and porous. Many species of swarming bacteria produce and secrete biosurfactants, which lower the surface tension of the swarm ﬂuid and allow the swarm front to advance with less difﬁ- culty ( 10). Investigating these bacterial biosurfactants proves to be medically relevant, because many of them have been found to have antimicrobial properties ( 11,12).

Furthermore, bacteria undergoing swarming motility have been observed to exhibit enhanced antibiotic resistance (13). An explanation for this observation is that because Submitted July 7, 2016, and accepted for publication February 16, 2017. *Correspondence:  Editor: Julie Biteen. 1462 Biophysical Journal 112, 1462–1471, April 11, 2017 http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.bpj.2017.02.019 /C2112017 Biophysical Society. these biosurfactants have antimicrobial properties, anti- biotic resistance genes are expressed during swarming to avoid autotoxicity. In addition, rhamnolipids, the bio- surfactants produced by P . aeruginosa, have been shown to have implications in bioremediation, as they increase the solubility and dispersion of certain hydrocarbons Because a bacterial swarm usually spreads outwards from a single point of inoculation, it ostensibly seems to be an example of chemotaxis.

Indeed, some bacteria have been shown to be chemotactic ( 16) or phototactic ( 17) during swarming. However, chemotaxis is not necessarily respon- sible for swarming motility. Swimming bacteria undergo chemotaxis individually, either by performing biased run- and-tumble motions, such as in the case of E. coli (18), or, if the cell only has a single ﬂagellum, performing repeated forward-backward-ﬂick maneuvers ( 19,20). In contrast, swarming bacteria constantly collide with their neighbors, so their movement is collectively controlled, as shown for swarms formed by E. coli (21) and by Bacillus subtilis (22). Lastly, strains of Proteus mirabilis that are nonchemo- tactic are still able to swarm ( 23). As bacteria swarm over an agar plate, they spread from a point of inoculation. The simplest pattern is a featureless swarm that expands outward equally in all directions.

The simplest pattern is a featureless swarm that expands outward equally in all directions. An example of this is an E. coli swarm, the edge of which is nearly transparent. It consists of a monolayer of bacteria, packed into a high, uniform density ( 24). In contrast, P . aeruginosa is known for its notably dendritic swarming pattern. These dendrites, or tendrils, are long, thin regions of colonization that emerge from the spot of inoculation or from other dendrites ( 25–27). Although there have been numerous studies that characterize the swarm morphology of P . aeruginosa strains under varied physicochemical conditions ( 25,26,28–33), the exact mechanism of den- dritic growth remains an open question despite several models proposed ( 25,28,29,34,35). In addition, although P .

In addition, although P . aeruginosadoes not swarm as a monolayer ( 36), the cells close to the agar surface can use type IV pili to move via twitching motility, in which the bacteria extend and retract their pili on surfaces, especially on harder substrate (~1% agar) (37). Thus, the presence of type IV pili may interfere with the swarming motility in P . aeruginosa, consistent with previous observations ( 31,38). In this work, we explore how numerous physical effects, including surface tension, osmotic drive, permeability, viscous drag, and viscous ﬁngering, are all coupled with the collective motion of P . aeruginosato account for varia- tions in swarming motility. Our guiding hypothesis is that the bacterial swarming behavior largely depends on ﬂuid and interface physics.

Our guiding hypothesis is that the bacterial swarming behavior largely depends on ﬂuid and interface physics. In particular, we propose the applica- tion of Darcy’s Law to account for the effects of physical factors such as osmotic pressure, matrix permeability, and medium viscosity on swarm motility via their inﬂuence on ﬂuid ﬂow. Darcy’s Law describes the ﬂow of a ﬂuid, Q (in the units of m 3/s), through a porous medium ( 39): Q ¼ /C0 kA ðDP /C0 DPÞ where k is the intrinsic permeability of the medium (with units of area, m2), A is the area of ﬂow (m2), DP is the difference in hydraulic pressure (Pa),DP is the difference in osmotic pres- sure (Pa), h is the ﬂuid viscosity (Pa ,s), and L is the length over which the total pressure drop is taking place.

Because the bodies of bacteria consist of water and solid materials assembled from nutrient molecules from the agar, the entire volume of bacteria and their surrounding ﬂuid must ultimately come out of the agar. Therefore, the volume increase of a bacterial swarm can be approximated as the total ﬂuid ﬂow Q out of the agar matrix. It is therefore a function of all the parameters based on Darcy’s Law. First, because the hydraulic pressure difference is negligible on a shallow agar plate, the driving force of the swarm ﬂow must be a rise in the local osmotic pressure, DP, generated by the bac- teria through secretion of osmolytes. It has been shown that in a spreading swarm of E. coli , the osmolarity of the leading edge region is higher than that of both the surrounding area and the interior of the swarm ( 40). This ﬁnding suggests that the bacteria secrete osmolytes, increasing the osmolarity near the edge to draw water out from the agar.

These osmo- lytes might be high-molecular-weight lipopolysaccharides (LPS), which transiently facilitate water extraction from the agar (41). Second, the ﬂow is proportional to the perme- ability coefﬁcient k of the agar gel, which is the porous me- dium that is also the source of water. Increasing the percentage of agar decreases the permeability of gel matrix (42), leading to a decrease of water ﬂow at a ﬁxed pressure difference. This decreased water ﬂow will in turn negatively affect the bacteria’s swarm speed. Third, the ﬂow is inversely proportional to the viscosity of the ﬂuid contained in the permeable medium. Here, we focus our analysis on the ﬂow of water out of the agar matrix, rather than the lateral ﬂow within the swarm ﬂuid itself. This focus is based on the assumption that extracting water out of the agar matrix is more difﬁcult than the lateral ﬂow of the swarm ﬂuid.

Consistent with this model, the length over which the pres- sure drop takes place refers to the thickness of the gel, and the area refers to regions covered by the active population of the bacteria that generates the osmotic pressure, which drives the ﬂuid ﬂow out of the agar. In this work, we test the main hypothesis, namely, that bacterial swarming is largely governed by interfacial ﬂuid physics and Darcy’s Law, by varying several physical pa- rameters experimentally. Speciﬁcally, we observe that exog- enous addition of the nonionic surfactant Triton X-100 enhances the swarming motility of P . aeruginosa, measured by the surface area spread of the swarm. Increasing the per- centage of agar was observed to decrease swarming, but Physical Effects on Bacterial Swarming Biophysical Journal 112, 1462–1471, April 11, 2017 1463 some swarming can be restored through the addition of Triton.

By performing experiments with polymers added to the agar, we show how increased viscosity in the agar matrix retards bacterial swarming. Adding salt and sugar into the agar, we also ﬁnd reduced swarming speed and interpret this decrease as due to the role of the salt and sugar in reducing the osmotic pressure difference that drives the ﬂuid ﬂow out of the agar gel. Our ﬁndings conﬁrm that the most essential factor in swarming of P . aeruginosa is its ability to draw up water from the agar gel matrix and allow it to spread over the agar surface. Bacteria thrive and swarm in this thin layer of water. This central physical picture helps us understand most observed swarming prop- erties of P . aeruginosa, with strong implications for other species of swarming bacteria as well. MATERIALS AND METHODS Bacterial strains and growth conditions The strains used in this study were P . aeruginosa PAO1 (wild-type) and P .

aeruginosa PAO1 (wild-type) and P . aeruginosa PAO1 DPilA (a pili-less mutant). Bacteria were grown from frozen stock, kept in a /C0 80/C14 freezer, and spread on 1.5% agar plates containing tryptic soy broth (TSB) ( 26). The plates were incubated over- night at 37/C14 C and then stored at 4 /C14 C for use as stock plates over 2–3 weeks. Liquid TSB media were inoculated by picking colonies from the stock plates and were grown overnight in 25-mL ﬂasks on a rotary shaker. Agar plate preparation Agar plates for swarming were prepared as described in Tremblay and D/C19eziel (26) and Xavier et al. (27).

(27). They were based on M8 media containing 24 mM KH 2PO4,4 8m MN a 2HPO4, 8 mM NaCl, 1 mM Mg 2SO4, and 0.1 mM CaCl 2, supplemented with 0.1% Casamino Acids (Bacto Labora- tories, Pritchard, NSW, Australia) and 0.5% glucose. A quantity of 20 mL of molten agar was poured to each plate. Agar (Bacto Laboratories) percentage varied in the range of 0.4–0.8%, with default at 0.5%. The plates were set to gel at room temperature by being placed, uncovered, under airﬂow in a biosafety cabinet, for 30–60 min before inoculation. Modiﬁcations of swarm agar Selected chemicals were added into the swarm agar to vary parameters of interest.

Modiﬁcations of swarm agar Selected chemicals were added into the swarm agar to vary parameters of interest. For a number of tests, agar plates were infused with the nonionic surfactant, Triton X-100, at concentrations of 0.0002, 0.002, 0.02, and 0.2%, by mass. Unless speciﬁed otherwise, experiments in this report pri- marily used 0.002% Triton X-100 to augment swarming. At the levels used in the study, Triton X-100 is nontoxic to P . aeruginosa(43). To vary osmolarity, extra NaCl or sucrose were added. To increase the viscosity of the swarm ﬂuid, some of the deionized water was replaced with a concentrated aqueous polyethylene oxide solution during the preparation of agar medium. We used polyethylene oxide with a molecular mass of 400,000 kDa (Cat. No. 372773, PEO 400,000; Sigma-Aldrich, St.

No. 372773, PEO 400,000; Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO). Its viscosity and osmolarity have been characterized and reported in a previous work ( 44). Swarming assay We followed a protocol posted online by the Xavier lab for a swarming assay that was used in their published work (27). Brieﬂy, from the overnight culture of P . aeruginosa, 1 mL of the suspension was taken and the bacteria were spun down at 6000 RPM for 3 min. After discarding the supernatant, the bacterial pellet was resuspended in 1 mL of phosphate-buffered saline and resedimented. This washing step was repeated to ensure that there was no TSB medium left with the bacterial suspension. After the two steps of washing, 2 mL of the bacterial suspension was deposited at the center of each agar plate at room temperature.

After the two steps of washing, 2 mL of the bacterial suspension was deposited at the center of each agar plate at room temperature. After covering up the plates and waiting 10 min for the droplet to absorb into the agar, the plates were placed upside-down (to prevent condensation, which tends to form on the top cover) inside the incubator at 37 /C14 C for bacterial growth. Swarm plate analysis Photographs were taken of the swarm plates using either a model No. D3200 DSLR camera (Nikon, Melville, NY) with an 18–55 mm zoom f/5.6 lens (for Fig. 1), or a model No. 7D DSLR camera (Canon, Melville, NY) with a 50 mm f/1.8 prime lens (for Figs. 2 , 3, 4,a n d 5). Because P .

2 , 3, 4,a n d 5). Because P . aeruginosa typically swarms in a dendritic pattern, it is more difﬁcult to quantify the swarm content, as opposed to concentrically swarming bacteria, such as E. coli .W eu s e dam e t h o dd e v e l o p e db yT r e m b l a ya n d D/C19eziel (26). Speciﬁcally, the photographs were imported into Adobe Pho- toshop (Adobe Systems, Mountain View, CA) and thresholded into black and white pixels. After thresholding and cleaning up the images, the num- ber of pixels in the area covered by the bacteria were counted and used to calculate the area swarmed. Counting the total number of pixels covering the entire plate (8.5 cm in diameter) conveniently yielded the pixel-to-area calibration.

An exogenous surfactant enhances swarming motility The P . aeruginosa PAO1 wild-type and its pili-less mutant strain DPilA both display swarming motility ( Fig. 1). Start- ing from a small number of cells on a circular area spread by 2 mL of bacterial suspension, the cell population grew over time. Approximately 12–15 h after inoculation, the cells extend beyond the initial area spread over by the 2 mL ﬂuid drop and then swarm over a large petri plate. Without added surfactant, the wild-type colony extends numerous branches, covering only a small fraction of the gel surface. The mutant strain spreads more fully, covering more area than the wild-type strain. This observation is consistent with reports by Anyan et al. ( 45), Kuchma et al. ( 46), and Murray and Kazmierczak ( 47).

( 45), Kuchma et al. ( 46), and Murray and Kazmierczak ( 47). When the nonionic surfac- tant Triton X-100 was added into the agar, the wild-type cells spread over a larger area than without added surfactant. With the added surfactant, the swarm front protrudes with more numerous branches and most branches widen as they grow outwards ( Fig. 1 a). Addition of Triton X-100 also dramatically alters the morphology of the mutant swarms, although it does not appear to increase the area covered at these low surfactant concentrations ( Fig. 1 b). To quantify the effects of the surfactant on swarming, we performed another set of experiments, in triplicates, on plates with agar containing ﬁve different concentrations of Triton X-100. The results, as shown in Fig. 2 , show a dose-dependent increase of swarm area for both strains. Yang et al.

The results, as shown in Fig. 2 , show a dose-dependent increase of swarm area for both strains. Yang et al. 1464 Biophysical Journal 112, 1462–1471, April 11, 2017 The added surfactant of 0.02% or more brings the wild-type swarms to about the same surface coverage as that of the mutant strain. The beneﬁt of Triton X-100 on spreading sat- urates at ~0.02% for both strains. Swarms of both strains display branched structures under certain conditions, but with different features. A dendritic pattern of the wild-type swarms is best represented in Fig. 1 a. The morphology of the swarms is extremely sensitive to the moisture level on the agar surface, and to variance of even a small amount of Triton X-100 added, leading to some inconsistency among different sets of experiments, such as when images inFigs. 1 and 2 are closely compared.

1 and 2 are closely compared. Neverthe- less, the consistent key ﬁnding is that the added surfactant fa- cilitates swarming, resulting in wider branches and broader swarm front. Speciﬁcally, at 0.02% Triton X-100, the swarm front consists of fewer but larger dendrites, or ﬁngers, as shown on both ﬁgures. At 0.2% Triton X-100 (Fig. 2), the swarm front appears smooth and circular, obliterating dendritelike fea- tures. The morphology of the mutant strain swarms appears more stable. The swarm front of the mutant contains numerous branches with ﬁne structures. Only on the plates containing the highest surfactant concentration are the large ﬁngerlike protrusions formed instead of ﬁne branches. Increasing agar percentage reduces swarming motility We performed the next set of swarming assays while varying the percentage of agar in each plate from 0.5 to 0.75%, in intervals of 0.05%.

As the amount of agar increased, the amount of swarming decreased for both strains ( Fig. 3 ). Again, the DPilA strain was shown to be a stronger swarmer than the wild-type. This was evidenced by the DPilA strain being able to not only swarm a greater area than the wild- type at the same percentage of agar, but also swarm at higher percentages of agar in general. Adding surfactant to the agar allows both strains to spread with less resistance. This enhancement becomes necessary for swarming to persist at slightly elevated agar percentage. Rather than a dramatic drop to minimal swarming as agar percentage was increased from 0.5% to 0.55% without added surfactant, both strains continued to swarm at higher percentages of agar with 0.002% Triton X-100 added. The wild-type strain’s ability to swarm diminished as the agar concentration increased to 0.6%, whereas DPilA strain swarmed until 0.65% agar, once again demonstrating that the DPilA strain is a stronger swarmer.

At higher concentra- tions of agar, adding surfactant resulted in no signiﬁcant in- crease in colony size for either strain, which may be set by their similar rates of population growth that are unaffected by the surfactant added. Elevated osmolarity in agar decreases swarming motility Because the DPilA strain was shown to be the stronger swarmer, we chose this strain to explore the effects of osmo- larity and viscosity on swarming. Altering the agar contents to increase its osmolarity by 150 and 300 mOsm using either NaCl or sucrose, we note that the swarm area decreased with increasing osmolarity ( Fig. 4). Because NaCl dissolves into two ions, only half as much in molarity was needed to reach the same osmolarity as sucrose; hence, the molar concentra- tions of NaCl and sucrose differed by a factor of two be- tween them for close comparison.

There was no marked difference between the plates containing NaCl and the plates containing sucrose, suggesting that the decrease in swarming motility is likely due to a physical factor, namely, the osmotic pressure, rather than a speciﬁc chemical or biochemical effect. This decrease in swarming motility does not appear to affect the swarm morphology. P . aeruginosahas been reported to survive hyperosmotic shock up to 700 mOsm of osmotic pressure ( 26), so the FIGURE 1 Swarming of wild-type P . aeruginosa (a) and a pili-less DPilA strain ( b) over the course of 24 h. Nonionic surfactant Triton X-100 was added into the agar at two concentrations to observe its effects on the swarming of both wild-type and the pili-less strain. The agar percent- age was 0.5%. The plate diameter was ~9 cm.

The agar percent- age was 0.5%. The plate diameter was ~9 cm. Physical Effects on Bacterial Swarming Biophysical Journal 112, 1462–1471, April 11, 2017 1465 observed reduction in swarming motility due to increased osmolarity up to 300 mOsm is not attributable to loss in viability or cell death. It is still possible, however, that moderately elevated osmotic pressure might slow down the cell division and population growth, thereby slowing down swarming. Both an ionic solute and a nonionic solute were chosen, because in E. coli it has been reported that up- regulation of certain operons in high osmotic pressure due to ionic solutes occurred at a much higher rate than when the high osmotic pressure was caused by a nonionic solute (27). We did not see a difference between the ionic and nonionic solutes in this experiment. Sucrose was selected as the nonionic solute because it cannot be metabolized by P . aeruginosa.

Sucrose was selected as the nonionic solute because it cannot be metabolized by P . aeruginosa. Infusing high-molecular-weight polymers in agar decreases swarming Our next experiment tested whether increasing the ﬂuid viscosity within the agar matrix would affect swarm speed. To do that, polyethylene oxide with a molecular mass of 400,000 kDa (henceforth referred to as ‘ ‘PEO 400,000’ ’) was added to the swarming agar. At 0.25% PEO 400,000, there was a small but marked decrease in the amount of swarming. At 0.5% PEO 400,000, the decrease in swarming became more apparent. However, addition of PEO 400,000 did not appear to severely affect the swarm morphology of P . aeruginosa. A wide variety of inert polymers were available at our disposal.

aeruginosa. A wide variety of inert polymers were available at our disposal. PEO 400,000 was chosen because adding it to a cell medium by 0.5% in weight increased its viscosity severalfold, to 4.9 cP , while causing negligible increase in osmolarity ( 44). Thus, adding this high-molecular-weight polymer at a low percentage allowed us to observe how the viscosity of the ﬂuid medium within the agar affected swarming independent of a signiﬁcant change in osmolarity of the same medium.
